title: Wave Propagation
description: Electromagnetic waves from scratch for complete beginners: 13 sub-topics from electric and magnetic fields and Maxwell's four rules through plane waves, materials, reflection, transmission lines, the Smith chart, waveguides, antennas, beamforming, radio propagation, link budgets and EM simulation. Physical intuition first with the heavy math turned into plain ideas; MCQ practice and a diagram in nearly every lesson, tiered easy to hard.

lessons.title: Electric Charge: Push and Pull
lessons.content:
Have you ever pulled a sock out of the dryer and had it cling to a shirt? Or felt your hair stand up after tugging off a wool hat? That little tug is **electric charge** at work — the same basic thing behind lightning, batteries, and (later in this course) radio and light.

**What is charge?** Everyday stuff is built from tiny particles. Some carry a property we call *negative* charge (electrons), and some carry *positive* charge (protons). Most objects have equal amounts, so they feel "neutral" — no net push. But rub two materials together and a few electrons hop from one to the other. Now one object has extra negative charge and the other has extra positive. We label them with a minus sign ($-$) and a plus sign ($+$).

**The one rule to remember:** charges push and pull on each other without touching.

- **Like charges repel** — two pluses push apart; two minuses push apart.
- **Opposite charges attract** — a plus and a minus pull together.

That is why a charged balloon sticks to a wall, and why static-clingy clothes grab each other. The pull or push is called the **electric force**.

One more everyday fact: the force gets **weaker as the charges move farther apart**, and stronger as they get closer. Hold the balloon near the wall and the tug is strong; pull it away and the tug fades.

**Recap:** charge comes in two kinds, plus and minus. Same kinds repel, opposite kinds attract, and the force weakens with distance. That simple push-and-pull is the seed of everything else in this topic.

items.prompt: Electric charge comes in how many kinds, and what are they called?
choices: One kind, called neutral
Two kinds, called positive and negative
Three kinds, called positive, negative, and neutral
Four kinds, one for each direction
items.explanation: There are exactly two kinds of charge: positive (written +) and negative (written -). 'Neutral' is not a third kind of charge — it just means an object has equal amounts of + and -, so they cancel out.

items.prompt: Two objects both carry extra positive charge. What do they do to each other?
choices: They pull together (attract)
They push apart (repel)
They ignore each other
They swap their charges
items.explanation: Like charges repel. Two positives (or two negatives) push apart. Only opposite charges — one + and one - — attract.

items.prompt: A positively charged balloon is brought near a negatively charged spot on a wall. What happens?
choices: They repel, pushing the balloon away
They attract, pulling the balloon toward the wall
Nothing, because one is on a wall
The balloon loses all its charge instantly
items.explanation: Opposite charges attract. A positive balloon and a negative patch pull toward each other, which is exactly why a rubbed balloon sticks to a wall.

items.prompt: You slowly move two attracting charges farther apart. How does the force between them change?
choices: It gets stronger
It gets weaker
It stays exactly the same
It reverses and becomes a push
items.explanation: The electric force weakens with distance. Closer charges feel a stronger pull or push; farther charges feel a weaker one. Moving apart never flips attraction into repulsion — only the sign of the charges does that.

items.prompt: Why is an ordinary, untouched object usually 'neutral'?
choices: It contains no charged particles at all
It has equal amounts of positive and negative charge, which cancel out
It only contains positive charge
Its charges are frozen and cannot move
items.explanation: Neutral does not mean empty of charge. It means the positive charge (protons) and negative charge (electrons) are present in equal amounts, so there is no leftover push or pull.

items.prompt: When you rub a wool hat on your hair and your hair stands up, what has happened?
choices: Charge was created from nothing by the rubbing
Some electrons moved from one material to the other, leaving each with a net charge
The hair became heavier
The hat turned the hair into a magnet
items.explanation: Rubbing transfers electrons between the materials. One ends up with extra negative charge and the other with extra positive. Charge is moved, not made from nothing. The standing hair is like charges repelling each other.

items.prompt: A plus sign (+) on an object means it has, compared to normal:
choices: Extra electrons
A shortage of negative charge (it lost some electrons)
Extra weight
No charge at all
items.explanation: A + label means the object is missing some of its negative charge (it gave up electrons), so positive charge is left over. A - label means the opposite: extra electrons.

items.prompt: Which everyday situation is the clearest example of the electric force?
choices: A heavy box resting on the floor
A sock clinging to a shirt straight out of the dryer
A ball rolling down a hill
Water boiling in a pot
items.explanation: Static cling is the electric force in action: rubbing in the dryer leaves the fabrics oppositely charged, so they attract and stick. The other examples are gravity, motion, and heat — not electric charge.

items.prompt: Two charges feel a push without ever touching. Is that possible?
choices: No, objects must touch to affect each other
Yes — charges push and pull on each other across the empty space between them
Only if the air is humid
Only if both charges are negative
items.explanation: The electric force acts at a distance, across the space between charges, with no contact needed. (How that 'reach across space' works is the idea of a field, coming up in the next lesson.)

lessons.title: What Is a Field? The Electric Field
lessons.content:
In the last lesson, charges pushed and pulled across empty space without touching. How does one charge 'reach' another that is far away? The answer is one of the most useful ideas in all of physics: the **field**.

**What is a field?** A field just means *a quantity that has a value at every point in space*. Think of the temperature in a room: every spot has some temperature — warmer near the heater, cooler by the window. You could, in principle, write a number at every point. That map of values is a *temperature field*. Or picture the warmth filling the space around a campfire: strong up close, faint far away. A field is that kind of thing — a value spread through space around a source.

**The electric field.** A charge fills the space around it with an **electric field**: at every point, the field tells you which way another charge would be pushed, and how hard. We can draw it with **field lines** — arrows that show the direction of the push.

- Around a **positive** charge, the field lines point **outward** (a stray positive charge would be pushed away).
- Around a **negative** charge, the field lines point **inward** (a stray positive charge would be pulled in).

Two things to read from a field-line picture:

1. **Direction** — which way the arrows point.
2. **Strength** — where the lines are crowded together, the field is **strong**; where they spread apart, it is **weak**. So the field is strongest right next to the charge and gets **weaker with distance** — the same fading we saw with the force.

The charge is the *source*; the field is the influence it spreads around itself. A charge dropped into that field then feels a push — that push is the electric force from Lesson 1.

**Recap:** a field is a value at every point in space. An electric charge surrounds itself with an electric field that points out of plus and into minus, and that is strong nearby and weak far away.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is a 'field'?
choices: A flat grassy area
A quantity that has a value at every point in space
A single number that describes a whole object
A wire that carries electricity
items.explanation: A field is a quantity with a value at every point in space — like temperature throughout a room. The electric field assigns a push (direction and strength) to every point around a charge.

items.prompt: Which everyday example is most like a field?
choices: The temperature at every spot in a room
The total number of chairs in a room
The color of a single chair
The price of the room
items.explanation: Temperature has a value at every point in the room — warmer near the heater, cooler by the window. That 'value at every point' is exactly what a field is. A single total or one object's color is just one number, not a value spread through space.

items.prompt: Which way do electric field lines point around a single positive charge?
choices: Outward, away from the charge
Inward, toward the charge
In circles around the charge
They do not point any particular way
items.explanation: Field lines point out of a positive charge, showing that another positive charge placed nearby would be pushed away. Around a negative charge the lines point inward instead.

items.prompt: In a field-line drawing, what does it mean where the lines are crowded close together?
choices: The field is weak there
The field is strong there
There is no field there
The charge is negative there
items.explanation: Crowded (closely spaced) field lines mean a strong field; widely spread lines mean a weak field. Lines are most crowded right next to the charge, so the field is strongest there.

items.prompt: As you move farther away from a charge, the electric field around it:
choices: Gets stronger
Gets weaker
Stays the same everywhere
Disappears completely at a fixed edge
items.explanation: The field gets weaker with distance — the field lines spread out as you go farther away. It fades gradually rather than stopping at a hard edge.

items.prompt: What creates an electric field in the first place?
choices: Electric charge
Heat
Sound
Gravity
items.explanation: An electric charge is the source of an electric field; it fills the surrounding space with the field. (A moving charge also makes a magnetic field — that comes in a later lesson.)

items.prompt: Does an electric field exist in the space around a lone charge even if no other charge is nearby to feel it?
choices: No — the field appears only when a second charge shows up
Yes — the charge fills the space with its field whether or not anything is there to feel it
Only if the charge is positive
Only if the charge is touching something
items.explanation: The field is set up by the source charge throughout the surrounding space, present whether or not a second charge is there. A push (force) only appears when another charge is placed in that field.

items.prompt: The field lines around a negative charge point inward. What does that tell you?
choices: A nearby positive charge would be pushed away
A nearby positive charge would be pulled toward the negative charge
The negative charge has no field
Nothing can be read from the direction
items.explanation: Inward-pointing lines mean a stray positive charge would be pulled in toward the negative charge — opposite charges attract. The arrow direction is the direction a positive test charge would be pushed.

items.prompt: How is the campfire analogy a good picture of a field?
choices: The fire's warmth has a value at every point around it — strong up close, faint far away
The fire is a single point with no surroundings
The warmth is the same at every distance
The warmth only exists if you touch the flames
items.explanation: The warmth around a fire has a value at every point in the space around it, strongest nearby and fading with distance — just like a field. An electric field surrounds a charge in the same spread-through-space way.

lessons.title: Voltage and the Energy in a Field
lessons.content:
We have an electric field that pushes charges around. Pushing something takes effort, and effort means **energy**. This lesson connects the field to two everyday words: **voltage** and **energy**.

**Voltage as 'electrical height'.** Imagine a hill. A ball at the top can roll down; a ball at the bottom just sits there. Height tells you how much push is available. **Voltage** (also called *electric potential*) is the electrical version of height. A point at high voltage is like the top of a hill for a positive charge; a point at low voltage is the bottom. Just as a ball rolls downhill, a positive charge tends to move from high voltage to low voltage. The difference in 'electrical height' between two points is what a battery provides: a 9-volt battery keeps one terminal 9 units of electrical height above the other. Voltage is measured in **volts** (V).

**A gentle note on field strength.** How strong an electric field is at a point can be described as how steeply the 'electrical height' changes there — a steep drop means a strong field. The strength of an electric field is measured in **volts per meter** (V/m), which you can read as simply 'how strong the push is' over a given distance. You do not need any formula — just the idea: steeper change in voltage means a stronger field.

**Energy stored in a field.** Here is a key idea for the whole course: *a field holds energy*. The very space where a field exists is loaded with energy, like a stretched spring or water held behind a dam. The stronger the field, the more energy packed into that space. This is why a charged-up capacitor (a small component that stores charge) can deliver a spark, and — looking ahead — it is why electromagnetic waves can carry energy across a room, or across the galaxy: they carry their fields, and the fields carry energy.

**Recap:** voltage is electrical 'height,' measured in volts; positive charges move from high to low voltage. Field strength (volts per meter) says how strong the push is. And crucially, a field stores energy — the stronger the field, the more energy in that space.

items.prompt: Voltage is best thought of as the electrical version of what everyday idea?
choices: Weight
Height (like the height of a hill)
Color
Temperature
items.explanation: Voltage is like height. A point at high voltage is like the top of a hill, and a positive charge tends to 'roll downhill' from high voltage to low voltage, just as a ball rolls down a slope.

items.prompt: Voltage is measured in:
choices: Volts
Meters
Seconds
Degrees
items.explanation: Voltage (electric potential) is measured in volts (V). Meters measure distance, seconds measure time, and degrees measure angle or temperature.

items.prompt: Following the hill analogy, which way does a positive charge tend to move on its own?
choices: From low voltage to high voltage
From high voltage to low voltage
It stays put no matter what
Sideways, along the same voltage
items.explanation: Like a ball rolling downhill, a positive charge tends to move from high voltage ('high ground') to low voltage ('low ground'). Pushing it the other way, uphill, takes added energy.

items.prompt: What does a 9-volt battery provide between its two terminals?
choices: A difference in 'electrical height' of 9 volts
Exactly 9 electrons
A temperature of 9 degrees
A magnetic field of 9 units
items.explanation: A 9-volt battery keeps one terminal 9 volts of 'electrical height' above the other. That voltage difference is what pushes charge through a connected circuit.

items.prompt: The strength of an electric field is often given in 'volts per meter (V/m).' What does that number tell you?
choices: How strong the push is over a given distance
How heavy the charge is
How long the field lasts in seconds
The temperature of the field
items.explanation: Volts per meter is a measure of how strong the field's push is across a stretch of space — a steeper change in voltage over a short distance means a stronger field. No formula is needed; it is just 'how strong the push is.'

items.prompt: A central idea of this course is that a field stores something. What does it store?
choices: Energy
Weight
Sound
Nothing at all
items.explanation: A field stores energy. The space where a field exists is loaded with energy, like a stretched spring. This is why a charged capacitor can release a spark and why waves can carry energy across distances.

items.prompt: Two regions of space contain electric fields. One field is much stronger than the other. Which holds more energy?
choices: The region with the stronger field
The region with the weaker field
They hold exactly the same energy
Neither holds any energy
items.explanation: The stronger the field, the more energy packed into that space. So the region with the stronger field stores more energy.

items.prompt: Why can a charged-up capacitor deliver a spark?
choices: It has stored energy in its electric field, which it can release
It is heavier than the wires around it
It contains a tiny battery of acid
It magnetizes the air
items.explanation: A capacitor stores charge and, with it, energy in the electric field between its parts. Releasing that stored field energy quickly produces a spark. This 'fields hold energy' idea reappears when waves carry energy across space.

items.prompt: Looking ahead: how can an electromagnetic wave carry energy across a room?
choices: It carries fields with it, and fields hold energy
It pushes air molecules the whole way
It uses a hidden wire
It cannot carry energy at all
items.explanation: A wave carries its electric (and magnetic) fields along with it, and those fields store energy. That is how light and radio deliver energy across a room — or across space — with no wire and no air needed.

lessons.title: Magnetism: Magnets and Moving Charges
lessons.content:
You have met electric charge and its field. Now meet its close partner: **magnetism**. You already know it from fridge magnets and the compass that always swings to point north.

**Magnets and poles.** Every magnet has two ends called **poles**: a **north pole** (N) and a **south pole** (S). The rule rhymes with the charge rule from Lesson 1:

- **Like poles repel** — two norths push apart; two souths push apart.
- **Opposite poles attract** — a north and a south pull together.

**The magnetic field.** Just as a charge surrounds itself with an electric field, a magnet surrounds itself with a **magnetic field** — a value at every point telling which way a compass needle would turn. You can *see* this field: sprinkle iron filings around a bar magnet and they line up into curved arcs running from the north pole around to the south pole. Those arcs are magnetic **field lines**. A compass is simply a tiny magnet free to spin; it lines up with the field, which is why it points along Earth's own magnetic field toward the north.

**The big surprise: moving charge makes magnetism.** Here is the discovery that ties the whole subject together. A **moving** electric charge — that is, an electric **current** — creates a magnetic field around it. Run a current through a straight wire and a magnetic field wraps around the wire in circles. (Point your right thumb along the current and your fingers curl the way the field points — a handy 'right-hand' picture, no math needed.) A compass held near a current-carrying wire will swing, just as if a magnet were there.

This is huge: it means electricity and magnetism are not two separate subjects. A *still* charge makes an electric field; a *moving* charge makes a magnetic field too. They are two sides of one thing.

**Recap:** magnets have north and south poles; like poles repel, opposite poles attract. A magnet has a magnetic field you can map with iron filings or a compass. And crucially, a moving charge (a current) creates a magnetic field — the first hint that electricity and magnetism are linked.

items.prompt: What are the two ends of a magnet called?
choices: Positive and negative
North pole and south pole
Top and bottom
Hot and cold
items.explanation: A magnet's two ends are its north pole (N) and south pole (S). Positive and negative are the names for electric charge, not magnetic poles — though the attract/repel rule is similar.

items.prompt: You bring the north poles of two magnets together. What happens?
choices: They pull together (attract)
They push apart (repel)
Nothing happens
One pole turns into a south pole
items.explanation: Like poles repel. Two north poles (or two south poles) push apart. Only a north brought near a south will attract.

items.prompt: Why do iron filings sprinkled around a bar magnet form curved arcs?
choices: The filings are sorting by size
They line up along the magnet's field lines, making the field visible
They are being heated by the magnet
Gravity pulls them into arcs
items.explanation: Each tiny iron filing acts like a little compass and lines up with the magnetic field. The arcs you see are the magnetic field lines made visible, running from the north pole around to the south pole.

items.prompt: How does a compass work?
choices: It is a tiny magnet that lines up with the magnetic field around it
It detects heat from the north
It measures voltage in the air
It points toward the heaviest nearby object
items.explanation: A compass needle is a small magnet free to spin. It lines up with the magnetic field it sits in — normally Earth's field — so it swings to point along that field, roughly toward north.

items.prompt: Besides a magnet, what else can create a magnetic field?
choices: A still, unmoving electric charge
A moving electric charge (an electric current)
A change in temperature
A loud sound
items.explanation: A moving charge — a current — creates a magnetic field. A still charge makes only an electric field; it takes motion to produce magnetism. This is the key link between electricity and magnetism.

items.prompt: Run a steady current through a straight wire. What shape does the magnetic field take around it?
choices: It points straight out from the wire like spokes
It wraps around the wire in circles
It points along the wire in the same direction as the current
There is no magnetic field around a wire
items.explanation: The magnetic field circles around a current-carrying wire. The 'right-hand' picture helps: point your thumb along the current and your fingers curl the way the field wraps around.

items.prompt: You hold a compass next to a wire and then switch on a current. What do you expect?
choices: The compass needle swings, as if a magnet were nearby
The compass needle melts
Nothing, because only magnets affect compasses
The compass points straight down
items.explanation: The current creates a magnetic field around the wire, and the compass (a tiny magnet) responds by swinging. This famous observation showed that electricity and magnetism are connected.

items.prompt: What is a magnetic field, in the same sense as an electric field?
choices: A value at every point in space, telling which way a compass would turn
A single number describing the whole magnet
A kind of electric charge
A beam that only exists in a straight line
items.explanation: Like the electric field, the magnetic field is a value at every point in space — here, the direction and strength that would turn a compass needle. Both are fields: influences spread through the space around their source.

items.prompt: What is the big-picture lesson from 'a still charge makes an electric field, a moving charge also makes a magnetic field'?
choices: Electricity and magnetism are completely unrelated
Electricity and magnetism are two sides of one thing
Magnetism is just heat in disguise
Only magnets, never charges, can make fields
items.explanation: The same charges are behind both fields — still ones make an electric field, moving ones add a magnetic field. So electricity and magnetism are two sides of a single subject, electromagnetism.

lessons.title: The Link, and the Family of Waves
lessons.content:
We end with the idea that pulls everything together and opens the door to the rest of this course: electricity and magnetism are so tightly linked that, working together, they can travel through space as **waves** — the waves we call radio, light, and Wi-Fi.

**The two-way link.** You have already seen one direction: a moving charge (a current) makes a magnetic field. The deep discovery is the reverse as well — a **changing** magnetic field creates an electric field. Put the two together: a changing electric field makes a magnetic field, and a changing magnetic field makes an electric field. Each one, while changing, gives rise to the other. (This back-and-forth is exactly what spins a generator at a power plant: moving magnets near coils create electric currents.)

**The seed of a wave.** Now imagine wiggling a charge back and forth. Its changing electric field makes a changing magnetic field nearby; that changing magnetic field makes a changing electric field a bit farther out; and so on, leapfrogging outward through empty space. That self-propelling ripple of linked electric and magnetic fields is an **electromagnetic wave**. It needs no wire and no air — it carries its own fields, and (from Lesson 3) those fields carry energy. That is how sunlight crosses empty space to reach us.

**One family, many frequencies.** How fast you wiggle the charge sets the wave's **frequency** — how rapidly it oscillates. Remarkably, all of these are the *same* phenomenon, differing only in frequency:

- low frequency: **radio** waves (broadcast, Wi-Fi, microwaves)
- middle: **infrared** (heat) and then **visible light** (the colors we see)
- high frequency: **ultraviolet**, then **X-rays**

Lined up by frequency, they form the **electromagnetic spectrum** — one continuous family. Radio and the light from your lamp are the same kind of thing, just oscillating at wildly different rates.

**Where this is heading.** Everything here has been the plain-words version. The precise rules that make it exact — how a changing electric field makes a magnetic one and vice versa, and exactly how fast the wave travels — are captured in a famous set of statements called **Maxwell's equations**, the subject of the next sub-topic. For now, hold onto the picture: linked, changing fields ripple outward as one family of waves from radio to light.

**Recap:** a changing electric field makes a magnetic field, and a changing magnetic field makes an electric field; together they leapfrog through space as electromagnetic waves. Sorted by frequency they form one spectrum — radio, light, X-rays — and Maxwell's equations (next) make all of this exact.

items.prompt: You already know a moving charge makes a magnetic field. What is the key reverse fact in this lesson?
choices: A changing magnetic field creates an electric field
A still magnet creates an electric current by itself
Magnetism cancels electricity
Nothing can create an electric field
items.explanation: The reverse link is that a changing magnetic field creates an electric field. Combined with 'a changing electric field makes a magnetic field,' this two-way relationship is what allows waves to form.

items.prompt: What is an electromagnetic wave, in plain terms?
choices: A ripple of linked electric and magnetic fields that travels through space
A sound that needs air to move
A wire carrying current
A still magnetic field sitting in place
items.explanation: An electromagnetic wave is a self-propelling ripple of linked, changing electric and magnetic fields moving through space. Each changing field creates the other, so the wave keeps going — no wire or air required.

items.prompt: Why can an electromagnetic wave travel through empty space, like sunlight reaching Earth?
choices: It carries its own electric and magnetic fields, which keep regenerating each other
It rides on air molecules the whole way
It travels along an invisible wire
It is pushed by gravity
items.explanation: The wave carries its own linked fields, each changing field creating the next. Because it needs no medium, it can cross the vacuum of space. Sound, by contrast, needs air or another material.

items.prompt: Radio waves, visible light, and X-rays are best described as:
choices: Three completely different phenomena
The same phenomenon (electromagnetic waves) at different frequencies
Three kinds of sound
Three kinds of electric current
items.explanation: They are all electromagnetic waves; they differ only in frequency. Lined up by frequency they form one continuous family — the electromagnetic spectrum.

items.prompt: Order these from LOW frequency to HIGH frequency.
choices: Radio, visible light, X-rays
X-rays, visible light, radio
Visible light, radio, X-rays
Radio, X-rays, visible light
items.explanation: From low to high frequency: radio waves, then infrared and visible light, then ultraviolet and X-rays. Radio is the low-frequency end; X-rays are near the high-frequency end.

items.prompt: What sets whether a wiggling charge produces, say, a radio wave versus visible light?
choices: How fast the charge wiggles (its frequency)
The color of the wire
The weight of the charge
The time of day
items.explanation: The rate of wiggling sets the wave's frequency, and frequency is what distinguishes radio from light from X-rays. They are otherwise the same kind of wave.

items.prompt: What is the name for the full lineup of electromagnetic waves sorted by frequency?
choices: The electromagnetic spectrum
The periodic table
The sound scale
The voltage ladder
items.explanation: Sorted by frequency, all electromagnetic waves form the electromagnetic spectrum — one continuous family from radio at the low end to X-rays and beyond at the high end.

items.prompt: A power-plant generator makes electric current by:
choices: Moving magnets near coils, so a changing magnetic field drives a current
Heating a wire until it glows
Letting two like charges repel
Standing a magnet perfectly still next to a wire
items.explanation: A generator moves magnets relative to coils, so the magnetic field through the coils keeps changing. A changing magnetic field creates an electric field that drives current — the reverse link in action. A still magnet would do nothing.

items.prompt: This lesson gave the plain-words picture. What makes these ideas exact, and where is it covered?
choices: Maxwell's equations, in the next sub-topic
Newton's laws, covered here
Nothing makes it exact; it stays a rough idea
The periodic table, in chemistry
items.explanation: Maxwell's equations are the precise rules behind the linked fields and the speed of the wave; they are the subject of the next sub-topic. This lesson built the intuition those equations make exact.

lessons.title: The Big Picture: How a Wiggle Becomes a Wave
lessons.content:
Flip on a light, open a Wi-Fi page, get an X-ray at the dentist. These feel like totally different things. They are actually the **same** thing: an **electromagnetic wave**. This lesson is the big picture of how that wave gets made and why it travels on its own.

**The 1860s breakthrough.** A scientist named James Clerk Maxwell took everything people knew about electricity and magnetism and boiled it down to just **four short rules**. We will read the rules one by one in the next lesson. Right now, only the headline matters.

**The headline.** You already know (from the fundamentals topic) that electric and magnetic fields are regions of push and pull around charges and magnets, and that a *changing* one of them can stir up the other. Maxwell's rules say this clearly:

- A **changing electric field** creates a **magnetic field**.
- A **changing magnetic field** creates an **electric field**.

Put those together and something wonderful happens. **Wiggle a charge** (shake an electron back and forth) and you make a changing electric field. That makes a changing magnetic field. That makes another changing electric field. Each one keeps re-creating the other, and the disturbance **rolls outward on its own** — away from where it started, even through empty space. That rolling disturbance is the electromagnetic wave.

**Everyday picture.** Think of two kids on a seesaw, each one's push sending the other up, over and over, so the motion keeps going without anyone new pushing. Or a single flick of a long rope: you flick once, and the *bump* travels all the way down the rope on its own. The fields are like that — one flick, and the pattern travels.

**How fast?** When Maxwell did the math, the wave came out moving at about $c=3\times10^8$ meters per second — the **speed of light**. That was the jaw-dropper: light itself is one of these waves. Radio, Wi-Fi, microwaves, and X-rays are the very same wave, just wiggling at different rates.

**Recap.** Maxwell summed up electricity and magnetism in four rules. The key idea: changing electric and magnetic fields keep re-creating each other, so a wiggle becomes a self-feeding wave that travels at the speed of light. Light and radio are that wave.

items.prompt: What did Maxwell do in the 1860s that was such a big deal?
choices: He invented the light bulb
He summed up all of electricity and magnetism in four short rules
He proved magnets and electricity have nothing to do with each other
He discovered the first radio station
items.explanation: Maxwell's achievement was unifying: he showed that all the known facts of electricity and magnetism could be captured in just four rules. He did not build the light bulb or a radio; his contribution was tying the whole subject together.

items.prompt: What is the single key idea that links changing electric and magnetic fields?
choices: A changing electric field cancels out any magnetic field nearby
A changing electric field makes a magnetic field, and a changing magnetic field makes an electric field
Electric fields and magnetic fields can never exist at the same time
Only a magnet can ever create an electric field
items.explanation: This mutual creation is the heart of the whole topic: change one field and you generate the other. Because each keeps re-creating the other, the disturbance can keep itself going and travel as a wave.

items.prompt: You shake (wiggle) an electric charge back and forth. According to the big picture, what happens?
choices: Nothing leaves the charge; the effect stays right where the charge is
A self-feeding pattern of changing electric and magnetic fields rolls outward as a wave
The charge instantly turns into light
Only a magnetic field appears, and it stays put
items.explanation: Wiggling a charge makes a changing electric field, which makes a changing magnetic field, and so on. The fields keep re-creating each other and the disturbance travels outward as an electromagnetic wave.

items.prompt: What did Maxwell discover about light?
choices: That light is a kind of sound wave
That light is an electromagnetic wave, traveling at the speed c
That light has no speed at all and arrives instantly
That light only exists inside the human eye
items.explanation: When Maxwell worked out how fast the field-wave travels, the answer matched the measured speed of light, about 3x10^8 m/s. That revealed light itself is an electromagnetic wave. Light is not sound (sound needs air and moves far slower), and it does have a finite speed.

items.prompt: Which everyday picture best captures the fields 're-creating each other' so the wave keeps going?
choices: A rock sitting still at the bottom of a pond
Two kids on a seesaw, each one's push sending the other up over and over
A candle slowly melting down
A book lying flat on a table
items.explanation: The seesaw captures the give-and-take: each side keeps the other going. In the same way, each changing field re-creates the other, so the disturbance sustains itself. The still rock, melting candle, and resting book have no back-and-forth hand-off.

items.prompt: Roughly how fast does an electromagnetic wave travel in empty space?
choices: About 340 meters per second (the speed of sound)
About 3x10^8 meters per second (the speed of light)
About 100 meters per second
It depends entirely on how loud the source is
items.explanation: Electromagnetic waves travel at the speed of light, c, which is about 3x10^8 m/s in empty space. 340 m/s is the speed of sound, a completely different (and far slower) kind of wave.

items.prompt: Which of these are all the same basic thing -- an electromagnetic wave -- just at different rates of wiggling?
choices: Sound, heat from a stove, and gravity
Visible light, radio, Wi-Fi, and X-rays
Wind, ocean waves, and earthquakes
Electric current, water pressure, and friction
items.explanation: Visible light, radio, Wi-Fi, and X-rays are all electromagnetic waves; they differ only in frequency (how fast the fields wiggle). Sound, wind, ocean waves, and earthquakes are mechanical waves or other effects, not electromagnetic waves.

items.prompt: Why can an electromagnetic wave keep traveling away from its source on its own, even through empty space?
choices: Because air molecules push it along from behind
Because each changing field keeps creating the next one, so the disturbance feeds itself
Because something must keep wiggling the charge the entire time the wave travels
Because empty space is full of tiny springs that bounce it forward
items.explanation: Once started, the changing electric field makes a changing magnetic field, which makes another changing electric field, and so on. This self-feeding hand-off needs no air and no continued pushing, so the wave rolls on through empty space by itself.

items.prompt: A single flick of a long rope sends a bump traveling down its whole length. Why is this a good picture of an electromagnetic wave?
choices: Because the rope must be electrically charged to work
Because one disturbance at the start travels outward on its own, without you having to keep flicking
Because the bump moves at the speed of light
Because ropes and light are made of the same material
items.explanation: The point of the rope analogy is that one starting disturbance travels away by itself. Likewise, one wiggle of a charge launches a wave that carries on without continued effort. (The rope is just an analogy; it need not be charged, and it certainly does not move at light speed.)

lessons.title: The Four Rules in Plain English
lessons.content:
Now let us read Maxwell's four rules one at a time. We will skip the heavy math and say what each one *means* in plain words, with a familiar example. (A **field** is just a region of space where a push or pull would be felt; a **field line** is an imaginary line we draw to show which way that push points.)

**Rule 1 - Charges are the sources of the electric field.** Electric field lines **start on positive charges and end on negative charges**. Picture iron filings or little arrows pointing away from a + and toward a -. The takeaway: wherever you find an electric field beginning or ending, there is electric charge sitting there. Charges are the 'faucets and drains' of the electric field.

**Rule 2 - There is no magnetic 'charge'.** Magnetic field lines **never start or stop** anywhere; they always form complete **loops**. Every magnet has both a north and a south end, and you can never get a single, lonely north pole by itself. Cut a magnet in half and each piece grows its own new south and north. So magnetic field lines just loop around, with no beginning or end. (A lone magnetic pole is called a magnetic 'monopole', and rule 2 says we never find one.)

**Rule 3 - A changing magnetic field makes a curling electric field.** If the magnetic field near a loop of wire **changes** (a magnet moves closer, or its strength rises and falls), an electric field appears, curling around, and it can push current through the wire. **This is exactly how a generator works**: spin a magnet near coils of wire and the changing magnetic field drives the electricity that lights your home. (This rule is called Faraday's law.)

**Rule 4 - Electric currents AND changing electric fields make a curling magnetic field.** Two things can wrap a magnetic field around themselves. First, an ordinary **electric current** (charges flowing in a wire) - that part was known before Maxwell. Second, and this was **Maxwell's key addition**: even with no flowing charges, a **changing electric field** does the same job. In other words, a changing electric field **acts like a current** for making magnetism. (This added piece is named the 'displacement current', but you can just remember it as 'changing E acts like a current'. The whole rule is called the Ampere-Maxwell law.)

**Why rule 4's extra piece matters.** It is the missing link. Rule 3 already says changing magnetism makes electricity. Rule 4's addition says changing electricity makes magnetism. Together they let the two fields keep re-creating each other with nothing else around - which, as the next lesson shows, is exactly what lets a wave travel through empty space.

**Recap.** Rule 1: charges are where electric field lines begin and end. Rule 2: magnetic field lines only loop - no lone poles. Rule 3: changing magnetism makes a curling electric field (generators). Rule 4: currents and changing electric fields both make a curling magnetic field (Maxwell's key addition).

items.prompt: In plain English, what does Rule 1 (the electric-field rule) say?
choices: Electric field lines form closed loops with no ends
Electric field lines start on positive charges and end on negative charges
Electric fields can only exist inside metal
A changing magnetic field is what creates every electric field
items.explanation: Rule 1 says charges are the sources of the electric field: lines begin on + and end on -. Looping with no ends describes the MAGNETIC field (Rule 2), not the electric field.

items.prompt: Which rule tells us there is no such thing as a lone magnetic pole (a magnetic 'monopole')?
choices: Rule 1 (charges are sources of E)
Rule 2 (no magnetic charge; field lines only loop)
Rule 3 (changing B makes curling E)
Rule 4 (currents and changing E make B)
items.explanation: Rule 2 says magnetic field lines never start or stop; they always form closed loops. That is the same as saying there is no isolated magnetic pole. Every magnet always has both a north and a south.

items.prompt: You cut a bar magnet in half. What happens to the poles?
choices: You get one piece that is only north and one that is only south
Each half becomes a complete magnet with its own north and south
Both halves lose all magnetism instantly
One half keeps both poles and the other becomes neutral
items.explanation: Because magnetic field lines always loop (Rule 2), you can never isolate a single pole. Cut a magnet and each piece grows a new pair, becoming a full magnet with both north and south.

items.prompt: A generator makes electricity by spinning a magnet near coils of wire. Which rule explains this?
choices: Rule 1: charges are sources of the electric field
Rule 2: no magnetic monopoles
Rule 3: a changing magnetic field makes a curling electric field
Rule 4: a current makes a magnetic field
items.explanation: Spinning the magnet means the magnetic field at the coils is constantly changing. Rule 3 (Faraday's law) says a changing magnetic field creates a curling electric field, which drives current in the coils. That is the generator.

items.prompt: What was Maxwell's key addition to the rule about what makes a magnetic field?
choices: That magnets can never make electricity
That a changing electric field, even with no flowing charges, also makes a curling magnetic field
That electric current cannot make a magnetic field after all
That magnetic fields do not really exist
items.explanation: Before Maxwell, people knew an electric current makes a magnetic field. Maxwell added that a CHANGING electric field does the same thing, even in empty space with no current. In short, a changing electric field acts like a current for making magnetism.

items.prompt: The term 'displacement current' is just a name for which plain-English idea?
choices: Charges being physically pushed out of a wire
A changing electric field acting like a current to create a magnetic field
The current lost as heat in a resistor
The current that flows only in a magnet
items.explanation: Despite the fancy name, 'displacement current' simply means a changing electric field behaves like a current when it comes to making a magnetic field. No actual charges need to flow.

items.prompt: Match the description to the rule: 'a changing magnetic field makes a curling electric field.' This is:
choices: The no-monopoles rule
Faraday's law (changing B makes E) -- the generator rule
The charges-as-sources rule
The Ampere-Maxwell law (currents make B)
items.explanation: Changing magnetism creating a curling electric field is Faraday's law, Rule 3, the principle behind generators. Do not confuse it with Rule 4, which goes the other way (electricity making magnetism).

items.prompt: Match the description to the rule: 'electric field lines start and end on charges.' This is:
choices: Rule 1, the charges-as-sources rule (Gauss's law for E)
Rule 2, the no-monopoles rule
Rule 3, Faraday's law
Rule 4, the Ampere-Maxwell law
items.explanation: Field lines starting and ending on charges is Rule 1: charges are the sources (and sinks) of the electric field. The other rules are about magnetic loops, generators, and making magnetism.

items.prompt: Why do Rules 3 and 4 together matter so much?
choices: They prove that magnets and charges cannot coexist
Together they let changing electric and magnetic fields keep re-creating each other, even in empty space
They show that electricity always cancels magnetism
They explain only how batteries store charge
items.explanation: Rule 3 says changing magnetism makes electricity; Rule 4 says changing electricity makes magnetism. Chain them and the two fields sustain each other with nothing else around -- the setup for a self-traveling wave in the next lesson.

items.prompt: What does a magnetic field line always do, according to Rule 2?
choices: Start on a north pole and simply end in empty space
Form a complete closed loop, never starting or stopping
Begin on a positive charge
Point straight and never curve
items.explanation: Rule 2 says magnetic field lines always close on themselves in loops; they never have a loose start or end. Beginning or ending on a charge describes ELECTRIC field lines (Rule 1), not magnetic ones.

lessons.title: From Two Rules to a Traveling Wave (and Why It's Light)
lessons.content:
We have the four rules. Now we connect the last two and watch a wave appear - and see why Maxwell's result changed science forever.

**Chaining rules 3 and 4.** Remember:

- Rule 3: a **changing magnetic field** makes a **curling electric field**.
- Rule 4: a **changing electric field** makes a **curling magnetic field**.

So start with a changing electric field somewhere (from a wiggled charge). By Rule 4 it builds a changing magnetic field right next to it. That changing magnetic field, by Rule 3, builds a changing electric field a bit farther along. Which (Rule 4 again) builds another magnetic field farther still - and on and on. The pattern keeps **handing itself forward** into new space, like **relay racers passing a baton** down the track. That hand-off, marching outward, is a **traveling electromagnetic wave**. Nothing else is needed - no wire, no air. The two fields are each other's whole support system.

**The speed comes out to c.** When Maxwell put rules 3 and 4 together with the math, the speed of the wave was set by **two basic numbers that describe empty space itself** - one for how empty space responds to electricity and one for how it responds to magnetism. Crunching those two numbers gives a speed of about $c=3\times10^8$ meters per second. (In symbols people write $c=1/\sqrt{\mu_0 \epsilon_0}$, where $\mu_0$ and $\epsilon_0$ are just those two 'constants of empty space' - you do not need the formula, only the idea that c is fixed by what empty space is like.)

**The historic 'aha'.** That number, $3\times10^8$ m/s, was already known from experiments - it was the measured **speed of light**. Maxwell's wave traveled at exactly the speed of light. The only sensible conclusion: **light itself is an electromagnetic wave**. In one stroke this united optics (the study of light) with electricity and magnetism. It is one of the great unifications in the history of physics.

**The wave is transverse.** In the wave, the electric field and the magnetic field both point **sideways** to the direction the wave travels - and they are **at right angles to each other** as well. So you have three directions all perpendicular: the electric field one way, the magnetic field at $90^\circ$ to it, and the travel direction at $90^\circ$ to both. A wave whose wiggle is sideways to its motion (not back-and-forth along it) is called **transverse** - like the up-and-down ripples traveling along a flicked rope, not like a slinky pushed end-to-end.

**The whole spectrum.** Nothing in the rules fixes how *fast* the fields wiggle. Wiggle slowly and you get **radio waves**; faster gives **microwaves**, then **infrared (heat)**, then **visible light**, then **ultraviolet**, then **X-rays and gamma rays**. They are all the same electromagnetic wave at different frequencies, and in empty space they **all travel at c**. That single idea predicts the entire electromagnetic spectrum.

**Recap.** Rules 3 and 4 hand the fields forward like a relay, creating a wave that needs no medium. Its speed, set by two constants of empty space, comes out to the speed of light - revealing that light *is* an electromagnetic wave. The wave is transverse (electric and magnetic fields at right angles to each other and to the travel direction), and the same idea covers the whole spectrum from radio to gamma rays.

items.prompt: How do Rules 3 and 4 chain together to make a wave that travels?
choices: They cancel each other so nothing moves
A changing E makes a changing B, which makes a changing E farther along, and so on -- handing the pattern forward through space
They only work if the wave stays in one spot
They require a wire to carry the wave the whole way
items.explanation: Rule 4 turns a changing electric field into a changing magnetic field; Rule 3 turns that into a changing electric field a bit farther on. Repeating this passes the disturbance forward into new space -- a traveling wave, with no wire or medium required.

items.prompt: Which everyday picture best fits how the fields pass the disturbance forward?
choices: A wall that bounces everything straight back
Relay racers passing a baton down the track
A bucket of still water
A locked door that stops all motion
items.explanation: Each field hands off to the next, carrying the wave farther along -- just like relay racers passing a baton. The still water and locked door have no forward hand-off, and a bouncing wall describes reflection, not steady forward travel.

items.prompt: What sets the speed of an electromagnetic wave in empty space?
choices: How brightly the source glows
Two basic constants that describe how empty space responds to electricity and magnetism
The color of the person looking at it
How much air is in the room
items.explanation: The wave's speed comes from two fixed numbers describing empty space itself (written mu_0 and epsilon_0). Because those are constant, the speed is fixed at c, regardless of brightness, observer, or air.

items.prompt: Maxwell computed the wave's speed and got about 3x10^8 m/s. Why was this such a historic moment?
choices: It was far slower than anyone expected
It exactly matched the measured speed of light, revealing that light is an electromagnetic wave
It proved light could not be a wave
It showed the wave had no speed at all
items.explanation: The calculated speed matched the already-measured speed of light. The natural conclusion was that light IS an electromagnetic wave -- uniting the study of light with electricity and magnetism, one of physics' great breakthroughs.

items.prompt: In the formula c = 1 / sqrt(mu_0 * epsilon_0), what are mu_0 and epsilon_0?
choices: The mass and charge of an electron
Two constants describing how empty space responds to magnetism and to electricity
The width and height of the wave
The temperature and pressure of the air
items.explanation: mu_0 and epsilon_0 are the two 'constants of empty space' -- one for magnetism, one for electricity. Together they fix the speed of light. You do not need to manipulate the formula; the point is that c is set by what empty space is like.

items.prompt: In an electromagnetic wave, how are the electric field, the magnetic field, and the travel direction arranged?
choices: All three point the same way
The electric and magnetic fields are at right angles to each other, and both are at right angles to the travel direction
The electric and magnetic fields point along the travel direction
The electric field points along the travel direction, the magnetic field sideways
items.explanation: All three directions are mutually perpendicular: the electric field one way, the magnetic field at 90 degrees to it, and the direction of travel at 90 degrees to both. This is what 'transverse' means for an EM wave.

items.prompt: Calling an electromagnetic wave 'transverse' means:
choices: The fields wiggle back and forth along the direction of travel
The fields wiggle sideways to the direction of travel, like ripples on a flicked rope
The wave can only travel through metal
The wave has no direction at all
items.explanation: Transverse means the wiggle is sideways to the motion, like the up-and-down ripple running along a rope. A back-and-forth-along-the-travel wiggle (like a pushed slinky) would be 'longitudinal', which an EM wave is not.

items.prompt: Radio waves, visible light, and X-rays differ mainly in what way?
choices: They travel at completely different speeds in empty space
They are different kinds of wave with nothing in common
They are the same electromagnetic wave but wiggle at different frequencies
Only X-rays are real waves; the others are not
items.explanation: All three are the same electromagnetic wave, differing only in frequency (how fast the fields wiggle). In empty space they all travel at the same speed, c. Nothing in Maxwell's rules fixes the frequency, so the whole spectrum is allowed.

items.prompt: Order these electromagnetic waves from lower frequency to higher frequency:
choices: X-rays, visible light, microwaves, radio
Radio, microwaves, visible light, X-rays
Visible light, radio, X-rays, microwaves
Microwaves, X-rays, radio, visible light
items.explanation: Going up in frequency: radio, then microwaves, then infrared and visible light, then ultraviolet, then X-rays and gamma rays. They are all the same kind of wave; frequency is what changes across the spectrum.

items.prompt: Why can an electromagnetic wave travel through the empty space between the Sun and Earth, with no air in between?
choices: Because sunlight is carried by air molecules the whole way
Because the electric and magnetic fields support each other, so the wave needs no medium
Because empty space is actually full of water
Because the wave is pulled along by Earth's gravity
items.explanation: Each changing field creates the next, so the wave is self-supporting and needs no material to travel through. That is exactly why sunlight crosses the empty space from the Sun to Earth. Sound, by contrast, needs air and cannot cross a vacuum.

lessons.title: What a Plane Wave Is, and the Words We Use for Waves
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Stand on a beach far from where the surf breaks and watch the swells roll in: long, straight, parallel crests, all marching toward shore together. That picture is a **plane wave** — the simplest kind of traveling wave there is.

**Build.** A *wave* is just a disturbance that travels and carries energy without carrying the stuff it moves through along with it. The water bobs up and down; the swell moves forward. The high points are **crests**, the low points are **troughs**. "Plane" means the crests form flat, straight sheets — picture those parallel lines of swell — and the wave moves straight ahead, at a right angle to those sheets. Each flat sheet of crest is called a **wavefront**.

Waves are everywhere, so we have a small set of words for *any* wave. Picture a single up-and-down ripple frozen in a photo:

- **Wavelength** — the distance from one crest to the next crest. (How long one ripple is.)
- **Amplitude** — how tall the wave is, measured from the calm middle line up to a crest. Bigger amplitude means a stronger wave.
- **Frequency** — how many crests pass a fixed point each second. More crests per second = higher frequency. We measure it in hertz (Hz), meaning "per second."
- **Period** — the flip side of frequency: how many seconds between one crest and the next. Fast wave (high frequency) = short period.

A light wave or a radio wave from a faraway source acts just like those ocean swells: by the time it reaches you, its wavefronts are essentially flat. That is why the plane wave is the everyday model for any distant source.

**Recap.** A plane wave is straight, parallel crests all traveling one way. Wavelength is crest-to-crest distance, amplitude is height, frequency is crests-per-second, and period is the time between crests.

items.prompt: In plain terms, what is a plane wave?
choices: A wave whose crests form flat, parallel sheets all moving the same direction
A wave that only travels along a flat floor or surface
A wave that has no crests or troughs
A wave shaped like an airplane
items.explanation: "Plane" refers to the flat, parallel sheets of crest (wavefronts), all moving one way together — like long ocean swells far from shore. It has nothing to do with airplanes or floors.

items.prompt: What is the wavelength of a wave?
choices: The distance from one crest to the next crest
The number of crests that pass each second
The height of the wave from the middle to a crest
The speed at which the wave travels
items.explanation: Wavelength is the crest-to-crest distance — how long one ripple is. Crests-per-second is frequency, height is amplitude, and how fast it moves is its speed.

items.prompt: What does the frequency of a wave tell you?
choices: How many crests pass a fixed point each second
How far apart the crests are
How tall the wave is
How far the wave has traveled
items.explanation: Frequency counts crests passing a point each second, measured in hertz (Hz). Distance between crests is wavelength; height is amplitude.

items.prompt: The amplitude of a wave is a measure of its:
choices: Strength, shown by its height from the middle to a crest
Length, from crest to crest
Speed through the medium
Number of crests per second
items.explanation: Amplitude is the height of the wave from the calm middle line up to a crest. A taller wave is a stronger wave. The other options describe wavelength, speed, and frequency.

items.prompt: What is the period of a wave?
choices: The time between one crest and the next at a fixed point
The distance between two crests
The total time the wave exists
The height of one crest
items.explanation: Period is the time from one crest to the next passing a point — the flip side of frequency. A high-frequency wave has a short period.

items.prompt: A flat sheet of crest in a plane wave is called a:
choices: Wavefront
Wavelength
Trough
Period
items.explanation: Each flat sheet where the wave is all at a crest together is a wavefront. Wavelength is a distance, a trough is a low point, and period is a time.

items.prompt: If one wave has a higher frequency than another, what does that mean?
choices: More of its crests pass a point each second
Its crests are taller
It travels a longer total distance
Its crests are farther apart
items.explanation: Higher frequency means more crests pass each second. Taller crests would be larger amplitude; farther-apart crests would be longer wavelength.

items.prompt: Why is a plane wave a good model for light or radio arriving from a faraway source?
choices: By the time the wave reaches you, its wavefronts are essentially flat
Faraway sources only make flat objects
Distant waves have no wavelength
The wave speeds up over long distances and flattens out
items.explanation: A distant source sends out wavefronts that, after traveling far, arrive looking essentially flat and parallel — just like ocean swells far from where they started. Speed does not change to flatten them.

items.prompt: In the ocean-swell picture, the water bobs up and down but the swell moves forward. What does this tell you about a wave?
choices: A wave carries energy forward without carrying the material along with it
The water travels all the way to shore with the wave
Waves can only exist in water
The wave and the water always move at the same speed
items.explanation: A wave is a traveling disturbance: it moves energy forward while the medium (the water) mostly just bobs in place. The water itself does not ride along to shore.

lessons.title: Speed = Frequency × Wavelength, and Why EM Waves Are Transverse
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Here is the one rule that ties every wave word together, and it is simple enough to keep in your head: a wave's **speed equals its frequency times its wavelength**.

**Build.** Think of crests marching past you like fence posts on a moving train. If the posts are far apart (long wavelength) but lots of them pass each second (high frequency), the train is fast. Speed is just "how much length passes per second," which is exactly wavelength (length per crest) times frequency (crests per second). In symbols:

$$v = f\lambda$$

Here $v$ is the speed, $f$ is the frequency, and $\lambda$ (the Greek letter "lambda") is the wavelength. For light and radio waves traveling through empty space, the speed is always the same fixed number, the speed of light, written $c$ (about 300 million meters per second, or $3\times10^8$ m/s). So for those waves:

$$c = f\lambda$$

Because $c$ is fixed, frequency and wavelength are a seesaw: if one goes up, the other must come down. **Higher frequency means shorter wavelength.** Quick example: a radio station at a frequency of 100 MHz (100 million crests per second, $1\times10^8$ Hz). Rearranged, wavelength $=c/f = (3\times10^8)/(1\times10^8) = 3$ meters. Clean and simple.

Two more words for picturing waves: a **wavefront** is a sheet of crest (from the last lesson), and a **ray** is just an arrow pointing the way the wave travels, always at a right angle to the wavefronts.

Finally, a quick recap of something you have already met: an electromagnetic (EM) wave is made of an electric field (call it **E**) and a magnetic field (**B**) that keep regenerating each other as they travel. EM waves are **transverse**, meaning the fields wiggle *across* the travel direction, not along it. E and B point at right angles to each other, and both are at right angles to the direction of travel.

**Recap.** Speed = frequency × wavelength ($v=f\lambda$); for EM waves in vacuum the speed is fixed at $c$, so higher frequency means shorter wavelength. Rays point along travel, perpendicular to wavefronts, and EM waves are transverse with E ⊥ B ⊥ travel direction.

items.prompt: What is the master relation connecting a wave's speed, frequency, and wavelength?
choices: speed = frequency × wavelength
speed = frequency ÷ wavelength
speed = frequency + wavelength
speed = frequency − wavelength
items.explanation: Speed is how much length passes per second: wavelength (length per crest) times frequency (crests per second), so $v=f\lambda$. It is a product, not a sum or quotient.

items.prompt: A radio wave has frequency 100 MHz ($1\times10^8$ Hz) and travels at the speed of light $c=3\times10^8$ m/s. What is its wavelength?
choices: 3 meters
30 meters
0.3 meters
300 meters
items.explanation: Rearrange $c=f\lambda$ to $\lambda=c/f=(3\times10^8)/(1\times10^8)=3$ m. Dividing the two powers of ten leaves 3.

items.prompt: For light or radio waves traveling through empty space, the speed is:
choices: Always the same fixed value, the speed of light $c$
Higher for higher-frequency waves
Lower for longer-wavelength waves
Different every time the wave is sent
items.explanation: All EM waves travel at the same fixed speed $c$ (about $3\times10^8$ m/s) in vacuum, regardless of their frequency or wavelength. That is what makes $c=f\lambda$ so useful.

items.prompt: Since EM waves in vacuum all travel at the fixed speed $c$, what happens to wavelength as frequency increases?
choices: Wavelength gets shorter
Wavelength gets longer
Wavelength stays exactly the same
Wavelength becomes zero
items.explanation: With $c$ fixed in $c=f\lambda$, frequency and wavelength are a seesaw. If frequency goes up, wavelength must come down to keep the product equal to $c$.

items.prompt: An EM wave is described as transverse. What does that mean?
choices: The electric and magnetic fields wiggle across the travel direction, not along it
The wave travels sideways instead of forward
The wave has no magnetic field
The fields point in the same direction the wave travels
items.explanation: Transverse means the wiggling (the fields) is perpendicular to the direction of travel. A wave wiggling along its travel direction would instead be called longitudinal.

items.prompt: In an EM wave, how are the electric field (E), the magnetic field (B), and the direction of travel arranged?
choices: All three are at right angles to one another
All three point the same way
E and B point the same way, across the travel direction
E and B point along the travel direction
items.explanation: E and B are perpendicular to each other, and both are perpendicular to the direction of travel: E ⊥ B ⊥ travel. This is the transverse, mutually-perpendicular structure of an EM wave.

items.prompt: What is a ray, as used to picture a wave?
choices: An arrow showing the direction the wave travels, perpendicular to the wavefronts
Another word for a crest
The height of the wave
The number of wavefronts per second
items.explanation: A ray is simply a direction-of-travel arrow, always at a right angle to the flat wavefronts. It does not measure height or count anything.

items.prompt: Two EM waves travel through vacuum. Wave A has twice the frequency of Wave B. How do their speeds compare?
choices: They travel at the same speed, c
Wave A is twice as fast
Wave B is twice as fast
Wave A is four times as fast
items.explanation: All EM waves in vacuum travel at the same speed $c$ no matter their frequency. The higher-frequency wave instead has a shorter wavelength, not a higher speed.

items.prompt: A wave has wavelength 2 m and frequency 5 crests per second (5 Hz). What is its speed?
choices: 10 m/s
7 m/s
items.explanation: Use $v=f\lambda = 5 \times 2 = 10$ m/s. Speed is the product of frequency and wavelength, so 5 crests per second each 2 m long means 10 m of wave passes per second.

lessons.title: Polarization, Phase, and How a Wave Carries Energy
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Grab one end of a jump rope and shake it. Wiggle your hand up and down and the rope waves vertically; wiggle side to side and it waves horizontally. Same rope, same speed — only the *direction of the wiggle* changed. For an EM wave, that direction has a name: **polarization**.

**Build.** Polarization is simply which way the electric field (the **E** field) points as the wave goes by. If E always wiggles up and down, the wave is **vertically polarized**; if it wiggles side to side, it is **horizontally polarized**. Both of these are called **linear** polarization, because E stays on one straight line. There is also **circular** polarization, where the E field rotates like a corkscrew as the wave travels — a steady spin instead of a back-and-forth.

Why care? Because antennas and many devices only "see" a wave that matches their own polarization. A radio antenna mounted vertically picks up vertically polarized signals best; turn it sideways and the signal weakens. Polarized sunglasses block the horizontally polarized glare bouncing off roads and water while letting other light through. Match the polarization and you get the signal; mismatch it and you lose strength.

Next, **phase** — a plain idea about timing. Two waves are **in step** (in phase) when their crests line up; add them and you get a bigger wave. They are **out of step** (out of phase) when one's crest lines up with the other's trough; add them and they cancel, leaving little or nothing. This adding-up and canceling explains echoes that reinforce or dead spots where a signal mysteriously drops out.

Finally, energy. A wave carries energy, and a bigger amplitude carries more (which is why a loud sound or bright light is a bigger wave). As a wave spreads out from a source in all directions, that energy is smeared over a larger and larger area, so the **intensity** (energy arriving per unit area each second) drops with distance. Move twice as far from a lamp and it looks dimmer — the same energy is now spread over a much bigger surface. One subtle note: the everyday "speed" of a wave we have used (the speed of its crests) is more precisely called its **phase velocity**. For our purposes, it is just how fast the pattern moves along.

**Recap.** Polarization is the direction the E field points (vertical, horizontal, or rotating/circular), and matching it matters for antennas and polarized glasses. In-step waves add, out-of-step waves cancel. A wave carries energy set by its amplitude, and that energy spreads out, so intensity falls with distance.

items.prompt: What is the polarization of an EM wave?
choices: The direction the electric field points as the wave passes
The speed at which the wave travels
The number of crests passing per second
The distance between two crests
items.explanation: Polarization is which way the E field wiggles — up-down, side-to-side, or rotating. The other choices describe speed, frequency, and wavelength.

items.prompt: An EM wave whose electric field always wiggles straight up and down is described as:
choices: Linearly (vertically) polarized
Circularly polarized
Unpolarized
Out of phase
items.explanation: When E stays on one straight line (here, vertical), the wave is linearly polarized. Circular polarization is when E rotates instead of staying on a line.

items.prompt: In circular polarization, what does the electric field do?
choices: It rotates steadily, like a corkscrew, as the wave travels
It stays fixed pointing straight up
It disappears and only the magnetic field remains
It speeds up and slows down each cycle
items.explanation: Circular polarization means the E field rotates as the wave moves forward, tracing a corkscrew. Staying on a fixed line would be linear polarization instead.

items.prompt: Why does polarization matter for a radio antenna?
choices: An antenna receives best when its orientation matches the wave's polarization; a mismatch weakens the signal
Polarization sets how loud the radio plays
Antennas ignore polarization entirely
Matching polarization makes the wave travel faster
items.explanation: An antenna picks up a matching polarization strongly and a mismatched one weakly — a vertical antenna favors vertically polarized waves. It does not change the wave's speed or volume directly.

items.prompt: How do polarized sunglasses reduce glare?
choices: They block the horizontally polarized light that reflects off roads and water
They speed up the light entering your eye
They block all light equally
They change the color of the sky
items.explanation: Glare off flat surfaces is mostly horizontally polarized, so the glasses are built to block that orientation while letting other light through. This is a polarization filter, not a brightness or color change.

items.prompt: Two identical waves are 'in step' (in phase). What happens when they combine?
choices: Their crests line up and they add into a bigger wave
Their crests cancel and leave nothing
They speed each other up
They change polarization
items.explanation: In step means crest lines up with crest, so the waves reinforce into a larger wave. Cancellation happens only when they are out of step (crest meets trough).

items.prompt: Two equal waves meet exactly 'out of step,' so one's crest lines up with the other's trough. The result is:
choices: They cancel, leaving little or nothing
They double in height
They travel twice as fast
Their wavelength doubles
items.explanation: When a crest meets a trough, the up and the down cancel, so the combined wave nearly vanishes. This canceling is why some spots have a dead, signal-free zone.

items.prompt: As a wave spreads out in all directions from a source, why does its intensity (energy arriving per unit area) drop with distance?
choices: The same energy is spread over a larger and larger area
The wave slows down as it gets farther away
The wave loses its frequency over distance
Energy is destroyed as the wave travels
items.explanation: The wave's energy gets smeared over an ever-bigger surface as it spreads, so less arrives on any given patch — which is why a lamp looks dimmer from farther away. The speed and energy are not destroyed; the energy is just spread thinner.

items.prompt: Which wave carries more energy, all else equal?
choices: The one with the larger amplitude
The one with the shorter wavelength
The one with the longer period
They always carry exactly the same energy
items.explanation: A bigger amplitude (taller wave) carries more energy — that is why a loud sound or bright light is a bigger wave. Wavelength and period describe spacing and timing, not the energy on their own here.

items.prompt: We have been calling the speed of a wave's crests its 'speed.' What is the more precise name for that speed?
choices: Phase velocity
Amplitude
Frequency
Polarization
items.explanation: The speed at which the crest pattern moves along is more precisely the phase velocity. Amplitude, frequency, and polarization describe other features of the wave, not its speed.

subtopics.title: Propagation in Media: Lossy, Dielectric & Conducting

lessons.title: Waves Slow Down Inside Materials: The Refractive Index
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Dip a straw into a glass of water and look from the side: the straw seems to bend or break right at the surface. Nothing is actually bent. What changed is the *light* — it travels at a different speed in water than in air, and that simple speed change is behind a surprising amount of everyday physics.

**Build.** From the last lessons you know a wave has a **wavelength** (crest-to-crest distance), a **frequency** (crests passing per second), and a speed tied together by speed = frequency × wavelength. In empty space (a **vacuum**, meaning nothing is there), a light or radio wave always travels at the same top speed, called the **speed of light**, written $c$ (about 300 million meters per second). In a vacuum the wave never weakens on its own — it only gets thinner because it spreads out.

Step *inside* a material — glass, water, air, a wall — and things change. The wave travels **slower**, and (as we will see next lesson) its wavelength shrinks to match. Some materials also soak up, or **absorb**, part of the wave so it fades as it goes; we will get to that too.

The one number that captures "how much slower" is the **refractive index**, written $n$. It simply tells you how many times slower light goes in that material than in a vacuum:

$$\text{speed in material} = \frac{c}{n}.$$

A vacuum has $n = 1$ (no slowdown). Air is barely more than 1 (about 1.0003), so light is almost as fast as in vacuum. Water is about $n = 1.33$, and glass is about $n = 1.5$. So in glass light travels at $c / 1.5$, roughly 200 million meters per second — noticeably slower. A bigger $n$ means a slower wave.

**Recap.** In a vacuum, light goes at its top speed $c$ and does not weaken on its own. Inside a material it goes slower, by a factor called the refractive index $n$: speed $= c/n$. Vacuum $n=1$, water $\approx 1.33$, glass $\approx 1.5$. That speed change is what bends the straw — the topic of the next lesson.

items.prompt: What does the refractive index n of a material tell you?
choices: How many times slower light travels in it than in a vacuum
How bright the light is inside it
How much the material weighs
How many crests the wave has
items.explanation: The refractive index n is a 'slowdown factor': light in the material travels at c/n. A bigger n means the wave is slower. It says nothing about brightness or weight.

items.prompt: Glass has a refractive index of about n = 1.5. If c is about 300 million m/s, how fast does light travel in the glass?
choices: About 200 million m/s (c divided by 1.5)
About 450 million m/s (c times 1.5)
Exactly c, the same as in vacuum
About 150 million m/s (c divided by 2)
items.explanation: Speed in a material is c/n. So 300 million divided by 1.5 is about 200 million m/s. You divide by n, not multiply — a material can only slow light down, never speed it past c.

items.prompt: What is the refractive index of a vacuum (empty space)?
choices: Exactly 1
About 1.33
About 1.5
items.explanation: A vacuum has n = 1, because light there travels at the full speed c with no slowdown (c/1 = c). Water is about 1.33 and glass about 1.5; an index of 0 would be impossible.

items.prompt: Two materials: material A has n = 1.3 and material B has n = 1.9. In which does light travel faster?
choices: Material A, because it has the smaller index
Material B, because it has the larger index
They are equal, since both are above 1
It is impossible to tell from the index
items.explanation: Speed is c/n, so a smaller n gives a faster wave. Material A (n = 1.3) lets light go faster than material B (n = 1.9). A bigger index always means more slowdown.

items.prompt: In a vacuum, with nothing else around, what happens to a light wave's strength as it travels straight ahead?
choices: It does not weaken on its own; a vacuum does not absorb it
It quickly fades to nothing
It speeds up over distance
It changes into a sound wave
items.explanation: Empty space does not absorb a wave, so a beam does not weaken on its own there. (A wave spreading out in all directions still thins because the energy is spread over more area — but that is spreading, not absorption.)

items.prompt: Why does a straw look bent where it enters a glass of water?
choices: Light changes speed between air and water, which bends its path
The water physically bends the straw
Water has no refractive index
The straw gives off its own light
items.explanation: Light travels slower in water (n about 1.33) than in air, and that change of speed bends the light's path at the surface — so the straw only looks bent. The straw itself is straight.

items.prompt: Air has a refractive index of about 1.0003. What does that tell you about light moving through air?
choices: It travels almost as fast as in a vacuum
It travels at half the vacuum speed
It stops almost immediately
It travels faster than in a vacuum
items.explanation: An index just barely above 1 means almost no slowdown, so light in air is nearly as fast as in vacuum. Nothing can carry light faster than the vacuum speed c, so 'faster than vacuum' is impossible.

items.prompt: Which statement best sums up what changes when a light wave goes from empty space into a material like glass or water?
choices: It travels slower, and some materials also absorb part of it
It always speeds up and gets brighter
Nothing at all changes
It turns into a completely different kind of wave
items.explanation: Inside a material the wave slows down (by the factor n), and many materials absorb some of it so it fades. In a vacuum it stays at full speed c and is not absorbed. It is still the same wave, just slower.
